full_text:
Bhubaneswar: The Odisha government has suspended Chief Minister Mohan Charan Majhi’s “Jan Abhiyog Sunani” (Public Grievance Hearing) programme scheduled for May 18 and May 25 in view of the prevailing heatwave conditions across the state.
According to a statement issued by the General Administration and Public Grievance Department on Friday, the public grievance hearing programme, usually conducted on Mondays, will remain closed on both dates due to the intense summer heat.
The department said the decision was taken keeping in mind the prevailing heatwave situation and public convenience.
It further stated that the date for the next “Jan Abhiyog Sunani” programme will be announced later through newspapers and other mass media platforms.
The Chief Minister’s grievance hearing programme is a key public outreach initiative through which citizens directly submit complaints and grievances to the state administration.

The arrival of India’s crucial southwest monsoon may come earlier than usual this year.In a press release dated May 15, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) predicted that the monsoon is likely to reach Kerala on May 26, about six days ahead of its typical onset date of June 1.
The arrival of India’s crucial southwest monsoon may come earlier than usual this year.In a press release dated May 15, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) predicted that the monsoon is likely to reach Kerala on May 26, about six days ahead of its typical onset date of June 1.
Possible Early Arrival with Margin of Error
The IMD has said the southwest monsoon may reach Kerala on May 26, which is about six days earlier than usual. However, there is a margin of error of plus or minus 4 days, so it could arrive anytime between May 22 and May 30.
Relief Amid Severe Heatwave
The announcement comes at a time when many parts of India are facing intense heat. Rajasthan’s Barmer recently recorded a high of 47.3 degree celcius on May 11, while heatwave warnings continue in states like Maharashtra, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, and parts of southern India.
Why Kerala’s Monsoon Matters
Kerala is the first state to receive the southwest monsoon every year. Its arrival marks the end of the hot pre-summer season and signals the start of rainfall moving north across India, usually covering the country by mid-July.
How IMD Makes the Forecast
Since 2005, the IMD has used a statistical model to predict the monsoon’s arrival in Kerala. The model studies six key factors, including wind patterns, ocean conditions, temperature changes, and radiation levels. It has been mostly accurate over the years, missing only in 2015.
Track Record and Importance
Recent years show the model has been reliable. In 2024 and 2025, the monsoon arrived close to or slightly earlier than predicted. Early arrivals in recent years suggest a trend of timely or early monsoons, which is important for farming, water supply, and daily life in India.
